posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
I’ve been dipping my toes into Bordeaux 2006 recently and tried these three Saint Juliens in the process:

Lagrange 2006

Blackberry, violet, a hint of vanilla and some cedar on the nose, with a typically soft, velvety mouthful of the same, plus a strong hint of plums mid-palate and quite a fresh, persistent finish. The style is reassuringly classical, understated and elegant. The grip is good and the tannins in the background make this a good bet for the future, but with a few hours in the decanter, it already gives a lot of pleasure.

Not quite on the level of the best Lagranges, but better than the 2004 at the same age, this is a sterling effort from one of the most consistent producers I know - and excellent value, as always. 92 pts

Gruaud Larose 2006

The nose is classic Gruaud - spicy, brambly fruit with a hint of funk, but much less than in the olden days. The attack is bright, with again the usual brambly fruit, before a very clear, precise taste of blackcurrant and a rising middle section featuring the usual dark cherry.

So basically, a classic Gruaud drinking beautifully now. I doubt this will be a long-term wine, since there is a lack of body to it, but the fruit is still quite primary, so it should hold for a decade, maybe with some improvement along the way.

A smidgen better than the Lagrange, and for me better than GL 01, 02 and 04 at the same age, with better ageing potential than all three. 93 pts

Saint Pierre 2006

The nose was very appealing - violets, vanilla, creamy strawberry and hints of spices. In the mouth, rather lush notes of wild strawberry and blackberry, but with enough acidity to keep it on track, very beguiling and winsome, trying hard to please. After one glass, I found it a bit tiresome and gave up, coming back a couple of days later, when it had settled down somewhat.
This is more restrained than many “modern” wines from this period and there isn’t a surfeit of oak or concentration, but it is a little sweet, giving the impression of a higher alcohol level than the 13° it announces.

Compared to the previous wines, this was less fresh, more heady and to my taste, a bit vulgar. If you like that sort of style, worth a good 92 points.

I was disappointed by the St.Pierre but really enjoyed the Lagrange and the Gruaud. I normally find that I agree with Neal Martin’s assessments, but not concerning the Gruaud. I’m a fan of Gruaud and always have been, so perhaps I’m biased - I just love the taste and the 06 is a very good example of it. Both the Gruaud and the Lagrange are reassuringly classical in style and they are both good value at today’s prices.

It’s too early to really have any idea of the overall quality of the vintage but so far, I like what I’ve been drinking.

posts.cooked: Thanks for this Julian. I’ve enjoyed many Lagrange and Gruaud Larose bottles over the years, but haven’t had any 2006s. St. Julien produces such excellent values in Bordeaux!

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
Cheers all!

Thanks for this Julian. I’ve enjoyed many Lagrange and Gruaud Larose bottles over the years, but haven’t had any 2006s. St. Julien produces such excellent values in Bordeaux!

Lagrange is for me the best value CC - with only one exception (2010) the price I find it at has not changed since at least 1996! If anything, I suspect its price disserves its reputation.

Gruaud is an odd case - the price varies depending on the vintage and what critics have said, so it can be incredibly good value for what you get.

Do you have the 2006 GPL? was thinking of picking some up and remember it as very nice

The all-time greatest wine I have ever tasted from Saint-Pierre, this estate, the smallest of the grand cru classes of St.-Julien, has an opaque purple color and a spectacular nose of subtle charcoal, creme de cassis, blackberry, and incense. > Full-bodied, with striking intensity and flamboyantly rich, exuberant flavors bursting with extract, the St. Pierre has no hard edges, but rather massive, incredibly well-endowed blockbuster style, which should prove to be monumental> . Give it 6-8 years to take on more definition and calm down, but this is a 30- to 40-year wine. Bravo!

The all-time greatest wine I have ever tasted from Saint-Pierre, this estate, the smallest of the grand cru classes of St.-Julien, has an opaque purple color and a spectacular nose of subtle charcoal, creme de cassis, blackberry, and incense. > Full-bodied, with striking intensity and flamboyantly rich, exuberant flavors bursting with extract, the St. Pierre has no hard edges, but rather massive, incredibly well-endowed blockbuster style, which should prove to be monumental> . Ok, so we are on the same page! I remember reading that when I bought it, thinking that it sounded like the 00 - it just shows how one’s understanding can change. I had no insight whatsoever into what he actually meant, since I had never tried anything along the same lines. Reading that note in today’s context would have saved me some money. Of course, I haven’t tried it yet so I’m speculating, but if they can produce such a similarly “flamboyantly rich” wine in a year like 2006, I shudder to think what the 09 must be like.

posts.name: Ian Amstad
posts.cooked:
Thanks Julian for the really useful reference points.

I will look out for Gruaud 2006 at auction. The price seems to be creeping up inexorably here, to about £70 pb now.

I have for a while regarded 2006 as an awkward vintage taking a bit longer to come round than other similar non-trophy vintages.

Will look out for Lagrange too, but have mixed feelings about this estate. The 1996 vintage, for example, is so up and down.

GPL 2006 is no great shakes in my experience, but it has been a while since I tried one.

Sad that St-Pierre went over to the dark side. Better off with Gloria.

posts.cooked:
Thank you very much. Interesting Information about a “forgotten” vintage.
Love to hear GL 06 is a good value and almost drinkable. Will open my case next year.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
Thanks Julian for the really useful reference points.

I will look out for Gruaud 2006 at auction. The price seems to be creeping up inexorably here, to about £70 pb now.

I have for a while regarded 2006 as an awkward vintage taking a bit longer to come round than other similar non-trophy vintages.

Will look out for Lagrange too, but have mixed feelings about this estate. The 1996 vintage, for example, is so up and down.

GPL 2006 is no great shakes in my experience, but it has been a while since I tried one.

Sad that St-Pierre went over to the dark side. Better off with Gloria.

Over here, the Gruaud 06 now goes for between 50 and 60€, which is in line with other “classic” vintages.

I quite agree about the awkwardness that most 06s were showing, hence my relative delight at how some are coming round now. I started buying some following recommendations by others here, for my youngest daughter’s birth year collection. I’m glad I did, since she is the only one of her siblings to really get into wine, with a much better nose than I’ve got and a growing perception for the different tastes - she is becoming my BS detector!
For the last three years, 2006 has been the perfect ugly duckling vintage at auction, which few were bidding for - that is changing but there are still good deals to be had.

posts.name: Ian Amstad
posts.cooked: Yes thanks Julian. I remember doing a Pichon Baron vertical with Christian Seeley a couple of years ago, and he was almost apologetic about his 2006, which was surly next to his more exuberant, almost all-singing-all-dancing, showings from his earlier years like 01, 02, 04. I think there is some similarity with 06 red burgundy. I would not describe either left or right bank Bordeaux or red burgundy as ‘Cinderella’ vintages but they can be overlooked.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I drank a 2006 La Mission last night and ended up buying a case of magnums. It must be the richest, most powerful left bank 2006, but not to a fault despite the rather high abv. The aromatics are really beginning to open up age 15, with lots of blackberry and blackcurrant fruit mingled with the smoke, cigar and truffle aromas that one is looking for in this estate’s wines. There’s still plenty of tannin there, and while I don’t think it will be as long-lived as the 2005, I think a lot of people would guess it as 2005 in a blind tasting. Certainly, the price difference between the 2006 and 2005 shouldn’t be as big as it is.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked:
Parker gave the 2006 St. Pierre a 93, with this note:

A candidate for one of the finest St.-Juliens of the vintage, St.-Pierre’s 2006 is one of the smaller productions in this consistent appellation, making it difficult to find in the marketplace. Its deep opaque purple color is accompanied by notes of roasted herbs, charcoal, graphite, and extravagant creme de cassis and licorice. Full-bodied, powerful, deep, and rich with outstanding balance, purity, texture, and length, this stunning 2006 will be very long-lived.

Neal Martin, on the other hand, didn’t particularly care for it much. He did really like the 2009 at the last 10-year retrospective, though.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked: I think modern Gruaud (post-2000 vintages) has become underrated. They are really making excellent wines but not getting the critical props. They are lighter-bodied and less heavy/in-your-face than the top Bordeaux, but to me that’s a plus. They really have a lot of elegance and a classical left bank style while still being more substantial and complex than wines lower in the hierarchy, and they are very reasonably priced for the quality. I say this based on having tried 2004, 2010, and 2015 over the last year and being very impressed. To me they are outperforming wines like Leoville Barton that get more props, as well as a number of left bank wines that have gone over to the dark side with excessive extraction and so forth.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
I think modern Gruaud (post-2000 vintages) has become underrated. They are really making excellent wines but not getting the critical props. They are lighter-bodied and less heavy/in-your-face than the top Bordeaux, but to me that’s a plus. They really have a lot of elegance and a classical left bank style while still being more substantial and complex than wines lower in the hierarchy, and they are very reasonably priced for the quality. I say this based on having tried 2004, 2010, and 2015 over the last year and being very impressed. To me they are outperforming wines like Leoville Barton that get more props, as well as a number of left bank wines that have gone over to the dark side with excessive extraction and so forth.

Yes, I couldn’t agree more about GL’s QPR.

I can’t think of another CC whose price varies so much according to the critical response at release. Often the market corrects itself over time. For example, the 2010 was rated three points lower than Léoville-Barton by RMP - so it was released at around 60€, whereas LB was released at around 100€. Today, the Barton sells for around 110€ over here, the GL for around 100€, practically the same, making the GL a very good bet at release - in fact, one of the best of all the 2010s, which were often too expensive at release and which have not moved much since.

To be fair, I think in recent vintages, Gruaud has priced itself too high at release, producing the opposite effect, so it pays to choose the right time to buy, but the end result is that it can always be found cheaper than most of its peers.

Each to their own, but when I compare Gruaud to any other Saint Julien apart from Ducru-B and the Léovilles, it wins most of the time in most vintages. DB and LLC are in a different price category, but as to comparisons with the Léovilles, it’s really a question of taste preference - I’m not so keen on Poyferré, I love Barton, but if I had to choose a desert island Saint Julien, it would be Gruaud. It’s one of the very few wines that I buy every year.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked: Where Gruaud really shines to me is that in my experience they aren’t playing the “big fruit”/extraction game, which so many in Bordeaux really are even though they claim they aren’t. E.g. the 2015 Gruaud, an excellent wine which was $70/bottle back when I got it, is a fresh, pure, 13% alcohol wine that is comes off as classy but totally unforced and is already accessible to drink (although of course it isn’t showing secondary complexity, more primary fruit). I value producers like that more than ever.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Julian, my British Doppelgänger - except I have a lot more hair and muscles - you prompted me to pop a beloved Sociando, this time the 2006 Cuvée Jean Gautreau!

I have to say, and it pains me to say it, I think Sociando missed it here. I will admit that things could unfurl with time, and I will sit on my remaining bottles, but this thing is super tightly wound, lean and a bit hollow on the fruit-side, for a vintage that should show more than this. Showing more new oak than I prefer, as well.

Bummer.

Quite agree, Marcus - thanks - I shall have to try one of my 2015s.

Bummer.

Hair is overrated, my expert of the everglades! How dare you suggest you have more than me - mine is merely optional and I’m lucky enough to have several “looks”:

That’s very bad news about the Cuvée JG 2006, of which I bought several for my daughter’s collection. Sadly, it sounds rather like the normal cuvée which I last tried a couple of years ago, with the same hollowness of the fruit. Fingers crossed that it will improve!

posts.name: Steen T Olsen
posts.cooked: Deleted.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
Nice notes, Julian. Thank you for the TNs- nice to see the Saint Juliens performing well. Of 2006 Saint Juliens, after having read through some of my tasting notebooks, I have only tasted Léoville Las Cases (surprise, surprise) and ended up giving that bottle a respectable 93+ score. Best 2006 I have tasted from Bordeaux has been a spectacular La Conseillante in April 2016, which showered a fantastic display of fragrant and silky La Conseillante charm, fantastic precision and absolutely enormous length on the finish (97+ pts). “Highly recommended” is an understatement here, because that La Conseillante 2006 really took wine tasting to new heights that day in April 2016…

Cheers Steen! Happily, I have both LLC and La Conseillante!

As I promised, I opened a bottle of GPL 06:

Grand Puy Lacoste - Pauillac - 2006

Dark raspberry, cassis and cigar box aromas, then a bright attack of cassis, before moving on to dark raspberry flavours, a rising middle section of creamy blackberry, plum, and then back to the cassis for the long finish. Already absolutely delicious, great elegance but with an underlying power which bodes well for the future. One of the best “classic” vintage GPLs in recent times, in fact for me probably the best - better than 01, 02, 04 and 07 (plus 03, obviously). It’ll be interesting to try this next to the 05 in another couple of years. Compared to the other 06s tasted, this just pips them on the finish line - 94 pts

Yes, highly recommended. Strangely, the price here has spiked this year, up from around 40€ to at least 70€, sometimes more - no idea why. Maybe it’ll come down again this winter but even at 70€ I’m looking out for more.

posts.name: Christopher Foley
posts.cooked:
I’ve enjoyed a few bottles of '06 Ormes de Pez this year.

Julian - based on your photos, I’m guessing you aren’t the Julian Marshall I hear on weekends from the BBC, doing world news stories and interviews, that airs on a local public radio station. He sounds much more “British” than you look.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
Chris, I can confirm that I’m not that Julian Marshall! But the photos were in fact when we were preparing for a fancy dress party a few years back - I went as Ziggy Stardust while my wife impersonated Vivienne Westwood! In real life, as Robert rather unfairly pointed out (the cad!), I’m somewhat short of my own hair, so I have to use wigs - as Joey Ramone once said - “Hair today, gone tomorrow”!

Glad the Ormes de Pez was good - it’s an often underrated address.

title: Lagrange, Gruaud Larose, St.Pierre and GPL 2006
